quiz.title: Algebra 2: Unit 10 Quiz 4
quiz.subtitle: 10.4: Sampling Methods and Bias
quiz.subject: Mathematics
quiz.topic: Evaluating methods for collecting data and recognizing sources of error

questions.question: A researcher wants to study the sleeping habits of high school students. They assign every student a number and use a random number generator to select 50 students. Which sampling method is being used?
questions.explanation: A Simple Random Sample (SRS) gives every member of the population and every possible group of size $n$ an equal chance of being selected.

questions.question: A company wants to know how employees feel about a new benefit. They interview every employee in the Sales and Marketing departments. Which sampling method is this?
questions.explanation: Cluster sampling involves dividing the population into groups (clusters) and then selecting entire clusters to interview.

questions.question: To determine the popularity of a new meal deal, a fast-food manager surveys the first 20 customers who enter the restaurant. Why is this sample biased?
options: The sample size is too large
The question is a leading question
questions.explanation: Convenience sampling is biased because it only includes people who are easy to reach, failing to represent the entire population.

questions.question: A principal wants to ensure that students from all four grade levels are represented in a survey. She randomly selects 25 students from each grade. Which sampling method is this?
questions.explanation: Stratified sampling divides the population into groups (strata) based on a characteristic and then samples from each group.

questions.question: A news station asks viewers to visit their website and vote on a controversial new policy. What is the primary source of bias in this poll?
questions.explanation: Voluntary response samples are biased because people with strong opinions are the most likely to participate.

questions.question: A quality control inspector selects every 15th lightbulb off the assembly line to test for defects. Which sampling method is being used?
questions.explanation: Systematic sampling follows a fixed interval or rule, such as selecting every $n$th individual.

questions.question: A survey asks: 'Given that the current tax rate is already a burden, do you support a further increase to fund the park?' This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Leading questions use suggestive wording to influence the respondent's answer toward a specific conclusion.

questions.question: A university has 12,000 students: 8,000 undergraduates and 4,000 graduates. A researcher randomly selects 200 undergraduates and 100 graduates to survey. Which sampling method is this?
questions.explanation: The population is divided into strata (undergraduates and graduates), and a random sample is taken from each group. This is stratified sampling.

questions.question: A pollster calls 500 households, but only 50 people answer the phone and complete the survey. What type of bias is most prevalent here?
questions.explanation: Non-response bias occurs when a significant portion of the sampled population cannot be reached or refuses to participate.

questions.question: A surveyor at the mall wants to know how much people spend on clothes. He only surveys teenagers. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Undercoverage occurs when some members of the population are left out of the sampling process, leading to results that don't represent the whole group.

scoreMessages.90: Great eye for identifying bias and sampling techniques! You can distinguish SRS, stratified, cluster, systematic, and convenience samples, and spot voluntary response, non-response, undercoverage, and leading question bias.
scoreMessages.70: Good job. Key reminders: (1) Stratified = sample from every subgroup, (2) Cluster = sample entire subgroups, (3) Voluntary response = people choose to participate, (4) Undercoverage = part of the population is excluded from selection.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing. Focus on: (1) Simple Random Sample — every member has an equal chance, (2) Stratified — divided by characteristic, then sampled from each, (3) Cluster — divided into groups, then entire groups are selected, (4) Systematic — every $n$th person, (5) Bias types: convenience, voluntary response, non-response, undercoverage, leading questions.
scoreMessages.0: Review sampling methods: (1) SRS: random selection from the whole population, (2) Stratified: divide into subgroups, sample from each, (3) Cluster: randomly select entire groups, (4) Systematic: every $n$th person, (5) Bias: convenience, voluntary response, undercoverage, non-response, and leading questions all distort results.
